description: Curated clinical guideline corpus for Clinical Reference RAG. Each chunk is a self-contained, authoritative summary drawn from publicly-available consensus guidelines, published CMS documentation, or widely-cited peer-reviewed definitions. These chunks give Clinical Reference real clinical grounding for questions about sepsis, heart failure staging, CDI query practice, and MS-DRG coding — not LLM pretraining alone. Loaded into pgvector via tools/product_docs/load_product_docs.py --corpus clinical_guidelines_corpus.json with source_type='clinical_guideline'.

chunks.title: Sepsis-3: the 2016 consensus definition of sepsis
chunks.text:
What is Sepsis-3? What is the current definition of sepsis? What is the difference between sepsis and severe sepsis?

The Third International Consensus Definitions for Sepsis and Septic Shock (Sepsis-3) were published in JAMA in February 2016 (Singer et al., JAMA. 2016;315(8):801-810). They replaced the earlier 1991/2001 definitions that had used SIRS criteria.

Sepsis-3 defines sepsis as 'life-threatening organ dysfunction caused by a dysregulated host response to infection.' Organ dysfunction is operationalized as an acute increase in the Sequential Organ Failure Assessment (SOFA) score of 2 or more points consequent to the infection, where baseline is assumed to be zero for patients not known to have preexisting organ dysfunction.

Septic shock is defined as a subset of sepsis in which particularly profound circulatory, cellular, and metabolic abnormalities are associated with a greater risk of mortality than sepsis alone. Clinically, septic shock is identified by a vasopressor requirement to maintain a mean arterial pressure (MAP) of 65 mmHg or greater AND a serum lactate level greater than 2 mmol/L (18 mg/dL) in the absence of hypovolemia. These criteria result in an in-hospital mortality rate exceeding 40%.

Critically, Sepsis-3 ELIMINATED the term 'severe sepsis' as a distinct category. Under Sepsis-3, sepsis itself requires organ dysfunction, so 'severe sepsis' became redundant and was removed from the terminology. What used to be called severe sepsis is now simply called sepsis under Sepsis-3 nomenclature.

SIRS (Systemic Inflammatory Response Syndrome) criteria — temperature abnormalities, tachycardia, tachypnea, leukocytosis — were retained as useful bedside screening but are no longer part of the diagnostic definition of sepsis. Sepsis-3 introduced qSOFA (quickSOFA) as a bedside screening tool for patients with suspected infection: altered mental status (Glasgow Coma Scale < 15), systolic blood pressure ≤ 100 mmHg, or respiratory rate ≥ 22 breaths/minute. Two or more qSOFA criteria identify patients with suspected infection who are likely to have a poor outcome.

chunks.title: SOFA score (Sequential Organ Failure Assessment)
chunks.text:
What is SOFA? What is the SOFA score? How is SOFA calculated? What SOFA change meets sepsis criteria?

The Sequential Organ Failure Assessment (SOFA) score, originally the Sepsis-related Organ Failure Assessment, is a point-based scoring system used to track a patient's status during their stay in an intensive care unit and to assess the severity of organ dysfunction. It is a core component of the Sepsis-3 definition.

SOFA scores six organ systems, each on a scale of 0 to 4 (0 = normal, 4 = most abnormal), for a total score of 0 to 24:

1. Respiratory: PaO2/FiO2 ratio (higher is better). 0 = ≥400, 1 = <400, 2 = <300, 3 = <200 with respiratory support, 4 = <100 with respiratory support.

2. Coagulation: Platelet count × 10³/µL. 0 = ≥150, 1 = <150, 2 = <100, 3 = <50, 4 = <20.

3. Liver: Bilirubin mg/dL (µmol/L). 0 = <1.2 (<20), 1 = 1.2-1.9 (20-32), 2 = 2.0-5.9 (33-101), 3 = 6.0-11.9 (102-204), 4 = >12.0 (>204).

4. Cardiovascular: Mean arterial pressure (MAP) or need for vasopressors. 0 = MAP ≥70, 1 = MAP <70, 2 = dopamine ≤5 or dobutamine (any dose), 3 = dopamine >5 or epinephrine ≤0.1 or norepinephrine ≤0.1, 4 = dopamine >15 or epinephrine >0.1 or norepinephrine >0.1 (doses in µg/kg/min).

5. Central nervous system: Glasgow Coma Scale. 0 = 15, 1 = 13-14, 2 = 10-12, 3 = 6-9, 4 = <6.

6. Renal: Creatinine mg/dL (µmol/L) or urine output. 0 = <1.2 (<110), 1 = 1.2-1.9 (110-170), 2 = 2.0-3.4 (171-299), 3 = 3.5-4.9 (300-440) or urine output <500 mL/day, 4 = >5.0 (>440) or urine output <200 mL/day.

For Sepsis-3 diagnosis, a patient with suspected infection meets criteria for sepsis when there is an ACUTE change in total SOFA score of 2 points or more consequent to the infection (baseline is assumed to be zero in patients not known to have preexisting organ dysfunction). A SOFA score increase of ≥2 is associated with an in-hospital mortality rate greater than 10%.

chunks.title: qSOFA: quick bedside sepsis screening outside the ICU
chunks.text:
What is qSOFA? How is qSOFA used? What are the qSOFA criteria?

quickSOFA (qSOFA) is a bedside screening tool introduced with the Sepsis-3 definitions in 2016. It's designed to rapidly identify patients with suspected infection who are likely to have a poor outcome, without requiring laboratory testing. qSOFA is NOT a diagnostic criterion for sepsis — it's a screening alert that prompts a more detailed evaluation.

qSOFA uses three clinical criteria, each scored as 0 or 1:

1. Altered mentation: Glasgow Coma Scale less than 15 (any deviation from normal mental status)
2. Systolic blood pressure: ≤ 100 mmHg
3. Respiratory rate: ≥ 22 breaths per minute

A qSOFA score of 2 or more in a patient with suspected or confirmed infection identifies them as being at greater risk of a poor outcome (prolonged ICU stay or in-hospital death). Such patients should be further evaluated for organ dysfunction and may warrant escalation of care, increased frequency of monitoring, and referral for critical care consultation.

qSOFA is appropriate for use outside the ICU — emergency department, hospital wards, pre-hospital settings — where blood gas analysis and full SOFA scoring are not immediately feasible. For ICU patients, full SOFA scoring remains the standard.

A limitation of qSOFA is that it may miss patients with sepsis who don't meet two qSOFA criteria, particularly in the very early stages. It's a screening tool, not a diagnostic tool. Negative qSOFA does not rule out sepsis; positive qSOFA is a high-risk flag that warrants further evaluation.

chunks.title: ACDIS/AHIMA compliant query practice: what makes a query compliant
chunks.text:
What makes a CDI query compliant? What are leading queries? What are the AHIMA/ACDIS query guidelines?

The AHIMA/ACDIS 'Guidelines for Achieving a Compliant Query Practice' (most recently updated 2022) is the authoritative reference for how CDI specialists and coders should write physician queries. A compliant query must be:

1. NOT LEADING. A leading query suggests a specific diagnosis or directs the physician toward a particular answer. Queries must present clinical indicators and ask the physician to clarify, not tell them what to document. Example of a leading query (non-compliant): 'The patient has a hemoglobin of 7.2 and received 2 units of packed red cells. Please document ACUTE BLOOD LOSS ANEMIA.' Compliant version: 'The patient has a hemoglobin of 7.2 and received 2 units of packed red cells. Please clarify the etiology of the anemia, if known.'

2. BASED ON CLINICAL INDICATORS documented in the medical record. A query must cite specific findings from the chart — labs, imaging, vital signs, physician assessments, medications — that prompt the question. A query without clinical indicators is fishing and is not compliant.

3. OPEN-ENDED WHERE APPROPRIATE. Open-ended queries (which ask for clarification without offering choices) are preferred when the clinical picture is uncertain. Multiple-choice queries are acceptable when offering a reasonable range of possible answers AND when all clinically reasonable options are presented AND when options include 'clinically undetermined' and 'other.'

4. COMPLIANT WITH RE-QUERY RESTRICTIONS. Repeat querying on the same issue is generally not permitted unless new clinical information emerges. Persistent re-querying to obtain a specific answer is coercive and non-compliant.

5. PART OF THE MEDICAL RECORD. Queries must be retained as part of the legal medical record (or linked business record) and are discoverable in audit. Verbal queries must be documented in writing.

6. NOT TIED TO REIMBURSEMENT LANGUAGE. Queries must not reference reimbursement, DRG assignment, CC/MCC status, or financial impact. These considerations cannot appear in the query itself, even if they motivated the inquiry internally.

A query that fails any of these tests creates audit exposure. RAC (Recovery Audit Contractor) reviewers specifically look for leading queries as evidence of improper influence on physician documentation, and a leading query can result in recoupment of the associated claim payment.

chunks.title: Heart failure classification: acuity, type, ejection fraction
chunks.text:
How is heart failure classified? What are the types of heart failure? What is HFrEF vs HFpEF?

Heart failure classification in clinical documentation and ICD-10-CM coding requires specificity along two or three dimensions:

1. ACUITY: acute, chronic, or acute on chronic. Acute heart failure presents with rapidly-developing symptoms requiring urgent intervention. Chronic heart failure is a long-standing condition managed with outpatient therapy. Acute on chronic heart failure is decompensation of a previously-stable chronic condition. Documentation must state one of these three.

2. TYPE by ejection fraction: systolic (reduced ejection fraction, HFrEF), diastolic (preserved ejection fraction, HFpEF), or combined. Classification by LVEF (measured via echocardiogram, cardiac MRI, or angiography):
   - HFrEF (heart failure with reduced ejection fraction): LVEF ≤ 40%
   - HFmrEF (heart failure with mildly reduced ejection fraction): LVEF 41-49%
   - HFpEF (heart failure with preserved ejection fraction): LVEF ≥ 50%

3. FUNCTIONAL CLASS (NYHA): Used to describe severity of symptoms, but not required for ICD-10-CM assignment:
   - Class I: No symptoms during ordinary activity
   - Class II: Slight limitation, comfortable at rest but ordinary activity causes symptoms
   - Class III: Marked limitation, comfortable at rest but less than ordinary activity causes symptoms
   - Class IV: Symptoms at rest, any physical activity increases discomfort

ICD-10-CM heart failure codes (I50.x) combine type and acuity:
- I50.20 Unspecified systolic (congestive) heart failure
- I50.21 Acute systolic (congestive) heart failure
- I50.22 Chronic systolic (congestive) heart failure
- I50.23 Acute on chronic systolic (congestive) heart failure
- I50.30-I50.33 Same pattern for diastolic heart failure
- I50.40-I50.43 Same pattern for combined systolic and diastolic
- I50.9 Heart failure, unspecified (non-specific, lacks documentation)

Documentation using only 'heart failure' without type and acuity falls back to I50.9, which is NOT a CC and does not shift the DRG. Documenting acute systolic heart failure (I50.21) can shift the patient from DRG 293 (Heart Failure without CC/MCC, weight 0.5490) to DRG 291 (Heart Failure with MCC, weight 1.3049), a revenue difference of approximately $5,442 per case at the CMS national base rate.

chunks.title: CMS MS-DRG: how the Medicare inpatient payment system works
chunks.text:
What is MS-DRG? How does Medicare pay hospitals for inpatient stays? What is a DRG triplet?

MS-DRG (Medicare Severity Diagnosis-Related Group) is the payment classification system CMS uses for Medicare inpatient hospital stays under the Inpatient Prospective Payment System (IPPS). Version 42 (MS-DRG v42) is effective for federal fiscal year 2025 (October 1, 2024 through September 30, 2025).

How MS-DRG assignment works: Each inpatient discharge is assigned to exactly one MS-DRG based on (1) the principal diagnosis, (2) any secondary diagnoses, (3) principal and secondary procedures, (4) patient age and sex, and (5) discharge status. There are approximately 770 MS-DRGs in the current version. Each MS-DRG has a 'relative weight' that reflects the average resource consumption for cases in that group compared to the national average (1.0).

Medicare payment to the hospital = MS-DRG relative weight × hospital base payment rate. The hospital base payment rate is adjusted for the hospital's wage index, teaching status (IME), and disproportionate share (DSH). A typical base rate in FY2025 is approximately $7,200 for a standard acute care hospital.

The CC/MCC triplet structure is critical for CDI: most clinical MS-DRGs come in groups of three variants distinguished only by whether the case has a qualifying secondary diagnosis. For example, heart failure DRGs 291/292/293:
- DRG 291 'Heart failure and shock with MCC' — weight 1.3049 (FY2025)
- DRG 292 'Heart failure and shock with CC' — weight 0.8613
- DRG 293 'Heart failure and shock without CC/MCC' — weight 0.5490

The same case shifts between these three DRGs based solely on whether the patient has a secondary diagnosis that CMS has designated as a Complication/Comorbidity (CC) or Major Complication/Comorbidity (MCC). The financial impact is significant: DRG 291 vs DRG 293 is a weight delta of 0.7559, which at a $7,200 base rate translates to a $5,442 revenue difference per case. This is why CDI specialists prioritize CC/MCC capture.

CMS publishes the complete list of CC and MCC codes annually in the IPPS Final Rule. FY2025 has approximately 3,334 MCC codes and 14,971 CC codes in ICD-10-CM. Noesis's specificity rules engine detects documentation that currently assigns to a non-CC/MCC code when the clinical evidence supports a more specific code that would qualify as CC or MCC.

chunks.title: CC vs MCC: the distinction that drives DRG tier assignment
chunks.text:
What is the difference between a CC and an MCC? What is a major complication or comorbidity?

In MS-DRG classification, CMS designates every ICD-10-CM diagnosis code as one of three categories for secondary diagnosis purposes:

1. MCC (Major Complication or Comorbidity): The highest-severity tier. A patient with at least one MCC as a secondary diagnosis is assigned to the 'with MCC' variant of their principal-diagnosis DRG, which has the highest relative weight in the triplet. Examples of MCC codes include sepsis (A41.9), acute respiratory failure (J96.00), acute kidney failure unspecified (N17.9), severe protein-calorie malnutrition (E43), and encephalopathy (G93.40).

2. CC (Complication or Comorbidity): The middle-severity tier. A patient without an MCC but with at least one CC is assigned to the 'with CC' variant, which has a lower relative weight than the MCC variant but higher than no-CC/MCC. Examples of CC codes include chronic kidney disease stage 3 (N18.3), moderate protein-calorie malnutrition (E44.0), unspecified pneumonia (J18.9), atrial fibrillation (I48.91), and congestive heart failure without further specification (I50.9).

3. Neither (non-CC/MCC): The lowest tier. If a patient has no CC or MCC, they're assigned to the 'without CC/MCC' variant, which has the lowest relative weight.

CMS publishes the complete CC and MCC lists annually in the IPPS Final Rule, Tables 6I (MCC) and 6J (CC). The lists change each year as codes are added, removed, or reclassified. FY2025 has approximately 3,334 MCC codes and 14,971 CC codes.

Only ONE secondary diagnosis is needed to shift the DRG: the presence of a single MCC bumps the case to the MCC tier regardless of how many CCs are present. Similarly, a single CC bumps the case to the CC tier if no MCC is present. This is why CDI specialists focus on CC/MCC capture — specifying one condition that moves the case from the non-CC/MCC tier to the CC or MCC tier can generate thousands of dollars in additional reimbursement for the same clinical care.

CC EXCLUSION LIST: CMS also publishes a CC exclusion list (Table 6K) that identifies principal diagnosis / secondary diagnosis pairs where the secondary diagnosis does NOT count as a CC/MCC for that specific principal diagnosis because the conditions are clinically related. For example, if the principal diagnosis is acute myocardial infarction, other closely-related cardiac codes won't bump the DRG tier because they're considered part of the same clinical picture. Noesis's DRG impact calculator takes these exclusions into account.

chunks.title: POA indicators and Hospital-Acquired Conditions (HACs)
chunks.text:
What is POA? What is the POA indicator? What is a Hospital-Acquired Condition? What is the HAC Reduction Program?

Present on Admission (POA) is an indicator CMS requires on every ICD-10-CM diagnosis code submitted on an inpatient Medicare claim. The POA indicator tells CMS whether the condition was present at the time of admission or developed during the hospital stay. Valid POA indicator values are:
- Y: Yes, present on admission
- N: No, not present on admission (developed during stay)
- U: Unknown, documentation is insufficient to determine
- W: Clinically undetermined
- 1 (or blank): Exempt from POA reporting

POA matters because of the CMS Hospital-Acquired Conditions (HAC) program. HACs are a specific list of conditions that CMS considers preventable through proper care and that, if they develop during the hospital stay (POA = N), CMS will NOT pay the increased DRG payment that the HAC would otherwise trigger. The current HAC list includes approximately 14 categories:

1. Foreign object retained after surgery
2. Air embolism
3. Blood incompatibility
4. Stage III and IV pressure ulcers
5. Falls and trauma (fractures, dislocations, intracranial injury, crushing injury, burns, electrical shock)
6. Manifestations of poor glycemic control (diabetic ketoacidosis, nonketotic hyperosmolar coma, hypoglycemic coma, secondary diabetes with ketoacidosis/hyperosmolarity)
7. Catheter-associated urinary tract infection (CAUTI)
8. Vascular catheter-associated infection (CLABSI)
9. Surgical site infection following CABG (mediastinitis), bariatric surgery, orthopedic procedures, cardiac implantable electronic device (CIED)
10. Deep vein thrombosis (DVT) and pulmonary embolism (PE) following certain orthopedic procedures
11. Iatrogenic pneumothorax with venous catheterization

WHY THIS IS A CDI SAFETY ISSUE: If a CDI query prompts documentation of a condition as present-on-admission when it actually developed during the stay, that's not revenue recovery — it's a False Claims Act exposure. A hospital that documents a HAC as POA when it wasn't has made a false claim for payment. This is why Noesis has a POA/HAC awareness layer: before surfacing a specificity gap for a condition on the HAC list, Noesis should check whether clinical evidence supports POA status, and if the condition looks hospital-acquired, route it to a quality/safety flag instead of a revenue opportunity.

The CMS HAC Reduction Program separately reduces Medicare payments by 1% for hospitals in the worst-performing quartile on a composite HAC score. This is a payment adjustment, not a per-claim action, but it creates additional incentive for hospitals to accurately document POA status and reduce preventable HACs.

chunks.title: ICD-10-CM coding principles and specificity
chunks.text:
What are the ICD-10-CM coding guidelines? What does specificity mean in coding? Why does unspecified code matter?

ICD-10-CM is the U.S. clinical modification of the WHO's International Classification of Diseases, Tenth Revision. It's the diagnosis code set used for billing, quality reporting, and epidemiological tracking. The Official Guidelines for Coding and Reporting are published annually by CMS, CDC/NCHS, AHA, and AHIMA (the four 'Cooperating Parties').

Key coding principles relevant to CDI:

1. CODE TO THE HIGHEST LEVEL OF SPECIFICITY. ICD-10-CM codes are up to 7 characters, with each additional character adding clinical detail (anatomic site, laterality, severity, encounter type). Coders must assign the most specific code supported by the documentation. Using a 3-character 'category' code when a 4-, 5-, 6-, or 7-character more-specific code exists is incorrect coding. Example: documentation that supports 'acute systolic heart failure' must be coded as I50.21, not the unspecified I50.9.

2. UNSPECIFIED CODES ARE NOT 'WRONG' BUT INDICATE MISSING DOCUMENTATION. Codes ending in .9 (e.g., I50.9, J18.9, N17.9, A41.9) are 'unspecified' variants used when documentation doesn't support a more specific code. They're valid codes, but they reflect a gap in clinical documentation. CDI programs focus on reducing unspecified code usage by querying physicians to clarify documentation where a more specific code is clinically supported.

3. CODE ALL REPORTABLE SECONDARY DIAGNOSES. A secondary diagnosis is reportable if it meets at least one of five criteria:
   - Clinically evaluated
   - Therapeutically treated
   - Diagnostic procedures performed
   - Extended length of stay
   - Increased nursing care or monitoring

This is sometimes called the 'MEAT' criteria (Monitored, Evaluated, Assessed/Addressed, or Treated). A condition that appears in the history but receives no current clinical attention during the stay is generally NOT reportable as a secondary diagnosis.

4. FOLLOW THE SEQUENCING RULES. The principal diagnosis is the condition 'established after study to be chiefly responsible for occasioning the admission.' It must be sequenced first. Secondary diagnoses follow in order of clinical significance.

5. DOCUMENTATION BY QUALIFIED PROVIDERS. Only documentation from qualified providers (physicians, NPs, PAs, CNMs) can be used for code assignment. Nursing documentation, social work notes, and dietary assessments can inform but cannot independently support code assignment. BMI, pressure ulcer stage, and other conditions do have specific exceptions where non-physician documentation can be used.

6. AVOID MIRRORING. Coders and CDI specialists must code what's documented, not what they think 'should' be documented based on clinical evidence. If the evidence supports a more specific diagnosis but the physician hasn't documented it, the appropriate response is a compliant query — not simply coding the more specific diagnosis on the coder's or CDI's own judgment.

chunks.title: RAC audits: what they look for and how good documentation defends against them
chunks.text:
What is a RAC audit? What do RAC auditors look for? How can good documentation help with RAC defense?

Recovery Audit Contractors (RACs) are private companies contracted by CMS to identify and recover improper Medicare payments. They review paid Medicare claims after the fact and look for both overpayments (where the hospital was paid more than it should have been) and underpayments (where the hospital was paid less). In practice, RAC focus is overwhelmingly on overpayments because recoupments generate revenue for CMS and the RAC itself (which is paid a contingency fee).

Common RAC audit targets:

1. DRG VALIDATION: RAC reviews whether the DRG billed is supported by the documentation in the medical record. If the record supports a lower-paying DRG, the difference is recouped. Leading queries, missing clinical indicators, and physician documentation that conflicts with the coded diagnosis are all red flags.

2. MEDICAL NECESSITY: For certain inpatient admissions (especially short stays, observation conversions, and specific procedures), RAC reviews whether the clinical documentation supports inpatient level of care. If it doesn't, the payment is recouped and the hospital may only be able to bill for outpatient services.

3. CODING ACCURACY: RAC looks for miscoded diagnoses, incorrect principal diagnosis selection, and inappropriate use of CC/MCC codes that don't meet reporting criteria (clinically evaluated, treated, etc.).

4. POA INDICATOR ACCURACY: Was the condition actually present on admission, or did it develop during the stay? HAC conditions that are improperly coded as POA can be recouped, and in egregious cases can trigger False Claims Act investigation.

5. QUERY COMPLIANCE: RAC reviewers can request copies of CDI queries to verify they were non-leading and based on documented clinical indicators. A leading query creates an audit trail that CMS can use to challenge the associated claim.

HOW GOOD CDI DEFENDS AGAINST RAC: The single best defense against a RAC recoupment is a complete, specific, evidence-grounded medical record. When the documentation clearly supports the billed DRG with explicit clinical indicators (labs, imaging, vitals, physician assessments), RAC reviewers have nothing to challenge. When the CDI program generates compliant queries that are preserved as part of the medical record, there's a clear audit trail showing the clinical indicators that prompted any documentation changes. Conversely, when documentation is vague, relies on unspecified codes, or shows evidence of leading queries, RAC recoupment becomes much easier for the auditor to justify.

Noesis's evidence chain (source text → SNOMED concept → ICD-10 code → DRG impact) creates exactly the kind of audit trail that RAC defense requires. Every specificity gap surfaced by Noesis includes citations to the clinical text that supports a more specific code, the ontology relationships that justify the mapping, and the compliant query template that would prompt the physician to clarify. If a hospital bills based on a documentation clarification that Noesis surfaced, the audit trail is complete from the moment the gap was identified.

chunks.title: When a secondary diagnosis is reportable: the MEAT criteria
chunks.text:
When is a secondary diagnosis reportable? What is MEAT? When can you code a chronic condition?

ICD-10-CM Official Guidelines for Coding and Reporting state that a secondary diagnosis is reportable only when it meets at least ONE of the following criteria during the current hospital encounter:

1. Clinically evaluated
2. Therapeutically treated
3. Diagnostic procedures performed
4. Extended length of stay
5. Increased nursing care and/or monitoring

The informal mnemonic for this in CDI training is 'MEAT' — Monitored, Evaluated, Assessed/Addressed, or Treated — which reframes the five criteria as actions the clinical team takes during the current stay.

Key clarifications:

- A condition listed only in the patient's past medical history without any current clinical attention is generally NOT reportable. For example, if a patient with a history of hypertension comes in for a hip fracture and their hypertension is stable and not addressed, hypertension would not be a reportable secondary diagnosis even though it appears in the problem list.

- A condition is reportable if the clinical team acknowledges it in their assessment, adjusts medications for it, orders lab tests to monitor it, or documents that they considered it in their treatment planning. Explicit documentation of evaluation, monitoring, or treatment is what makes it reportable.

- Chronic conditions require the SAME test. Diabetes, hypertension, CKD, COPD, heart failure — all chronic — are only reportable for this stay if they're being clinically evaluated, treated, or monitored during this stay. They don't automatically carry forward from the problem list.

- EXCEPTIONS: Some conditions are reportable even without explicit evaluation if they impact the current care. A history of MI or stroke, for example, often influences medication choices and can be reportable if it shapes the current treatment plan. HIV, malignancy, and other clinically significant conditions with long-term implications for care are generally reportable even when they're stable.

FROM A CDI PERSPECTIVE: This is the core of what CDI specialists do all day. They review the medical record to identify conditions that ARE being clinically evaluated, treated, or monitored but haven't been explicitly documented with the right specificity. A patient who is receiving daily furosemide for volume overload, getting daily weights and renal panel monitoring, with a BNP of 1200 and clear fluid retention — that patient has heart failure as an ACTIVE secondary diagnosis and it should be reported with full specificity (type, acuity) even if the attending physician hasn't explicitly written 'acute on chronic systolic heart failure' in today's progress note. The compliant response is a query to clarify, not independent code assignment.

Noesis's specificity rules engine is designed to detect exactly this pattern: when clinical evidence (meds, labs, vital signs, procedures) supports a condition as actively managed but the documentation lacks the specificity required for the most accurate code, Noesis surfaces the gap with the evidence chain and a compliant query template.

chunks.title: Case-Mix Index (CMI): what it measures and why CFOs care
chunks.text:
What is Case-Mix Index? How is CMI calculated? Why does CMI matter?

Case-Mix Index (CMI) is a single number that summarizes the average complexity of Medicare inpatient cases at a hospital. It's calculated as the weighted average of MS-DRG relative weights across all Medicare inpatient discharges over a given period:

CMI = SUM(DRG relative weight × discharges in that DRG) / SUM(total discharges)

A CMI of 1.0 means the hospital's case mix matches the national average. A CMI of 1.6 means the average case is 60% more resource-intensive than the national average — meaning the hospital is treating sicker, more complex patients on average. A CMI of 0.9 means the average case is 10% less resource-intensive.

For FY2025, the national median CMI across U.S. acute care hospitals is approximately 1.60, with the 25th percentile around 1.47 and the 75th percentile around 1.81. Academic medical centers typically have CMIs in the 2.5-3.0 range; community hospitals typically fall between 1.3 and 1.8.

WHY CMI MATTERS:

1. DIRECT REVENUE IMPACT. Medicare payment is approximately proportional to CMI. A hospital that raises its CMI from 1.50 to 1.60 (a 0.10 increase, typical of a year of strong CDI work) sees proportional revenue growth on Medicare inpatient cases. For a hospital with 5,000 annual Medicare discharges at a $7,200 base rate, that 0.10 CMI increase translates to approximately $3.6 million in additional revenue per year.

2. BOARDROOM VISIBILITY. CMI is one of the few hospital performance metrics that CFOs, CMOs, and board members all recognize and track. It's reported in board packages, benchmarked against peer hospitals, and factored into strategic planning. CMI improvement is a measurable, publishable, revenue-generating outcome of CDI work — which is why CDI departments are funded from revenue integrity budgets.

3. CDI PERFORMANCE METRIC. CDI program directors are often measured on CMI trend. A CDI team that improves documentation quality over time should see a corresponding rise in CMI (assuming the underlying patient population isn't changing dramatically). Conversely, a declining CMI can trigger audits or performance reviews of CDI operations.

4. PEER BENCHMARKING. CMS publishes CMI by provider, so hospitals can compare themselves to peers by region, bed count, teaching status, or ownership. A hospital with a CMI significantly below its peer group has either (a) a genuinely lower-complexity patient population or (b) a CDI opportunity — documentation that fails to fully capture the clinical complexity of cases being treated. This is the gap Noesis surfaces with its CMS Hospital Compare benchmarking.

LIMITATIONS: CMI reflects coded complexity, not actual clinical complexity. Two hospitals treating identical patients can have different CMIs if one has better CDI and documentation practices. This is both the opportunity (CDI improvements raise CMI and revenue without changing care) and the risk (inflating CMI through leading queries or improper coding is fraud).
